speaker: interviewer
text: --five. Interview with Paula Tencer. Tape number five.

speaker: interviewee
text: Laura was, being a Zionist, joined the organization. And she went to the hachshara preparing herself for future life in Palestine at the time. There was a communal life where they learned to share and to live-- get along together. She met a fellow, [? Motek. ?] He came from [INAUDIBLE], also Poland. Very handsome, very highly-educated, and from a fine family. And a romance developed, and they were very much in love. When she completed that hachshara, she came home. She was quiet, withdrawn. You could notice that she was engrossed in-- in some thoughts. She was writing letters, and rush-- rushing to the mailman to pick up her correspondence. She did not dare to tell us, to tell mother, about her love, because she-- she feared that we may treat it lightly. And to her, it was so important. Sometimes she received-- mailman brought mail, and she would sit in the garden and read. And I, even as younger, noticed tears in her eyes. This did not escape my mother's attention. She noticed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, why don't you invite that young man to the house? We should meet him. And Laura said, you mean it? You mean it? And my mother says, yes. I mean it. So Laura picked my mother up and whirled her around, laughing and crying. And it was decided that for the weekend, [? Motek ?] will come. And we started cleaning and scrubbing the house from head to toe, and polishing. And my mother cooked all her delicious food. And he's going to come for the Sabbath.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [? Motek ?] arrived. We awaited him at the station. Even though I was younger, I was aware of a handsome man. He was very handsome, tall, the bluest eyes, broad-shouldered. We loved him from the start. He came to the house, and that evening, my father took him to the temple with him and showed him off. My mother, the table with gleaming white with the candle-- candelabra lit, and all the delicious food. Every child scrubbed, ready. It was a holiday atmosphere. And when they came home, we sat down and ate. And after dinner, we were sitting, talking, and he said, she-- my sister had a leather jacket which was tight in the waist. So he jokingly he said, I don't know if I fell in love with that leather jacket, or with her. He-- he loved her, from the start. And they were talking seriously that he has to avoid-- he wants to avoid compulsory military service, which was for two years. He would like to go to Palestine before he is eligible for service to beat that deadline. He had a brother already in Palestine, an older brother. And he was the youngest. And the parents were not anxious to send him away. They had a daughter, an older sister, who was not married. And in Jewish tradition it was, the older sister has to get married first before you consider the-- it's like traditional. They were not-- but they saw that he's very-- and therefore, they were not encouraging him. They wanted him to be home, to stay home. And they were not that wealthy, but very respected, fine people. And he was very serious. And it was decided that we will supply-- well, they'll get married. We will supply his passage and wardrobe, what he needs. He should go to Palestine. It's a very hard life there. He will establish himself, and then send for her. Because it was a pioneer country. The climate is hot. It's hard. It was not the reason alone, but that was my father's logic-- give us the pleasure of having her here for a while longer. You'll establish yourself. In the meantime, she'll study Hebrew. She'll prepare herself, and then she'll come. And it was decided. Upon his return home, he told his parents. They were not very happy to send away their son to unknown. But they accepted it, and we made a small wedding just for the family. It was a beautiful wedding. And we had that little-- in the garden-- that little house. Laura converted it with organza curtains and a beautiful place. They spend their honeymoon there. And they were walking through the forests, and spending time just to them. And when they came home, there was a delicious supper. My mother never allowed her to do anything. She should just devote her time with [? Motek ?] was his-- [? Motek ?] Myers was his name. And then the time came for him to go. He has to go to prepare his parents to tell them. And he'll-- everything will be taken care of. We took him to the station, and Laura said goodbye and they kissed. And we were leaving, and then Laura turned around and, crying, ran again into his arms, saying-- kissing him. She cried all the way home. And my father said, don't cry, Laura. Your husband is going to build a future for you. You see. You apply yourself, get busy, and before you know, you'll be there, too. I wish I would have the funds now to send you both together. But my operation in Vienna and hardship now. So give us the pleasure of having you  a little longer. And under the circumstances, that was the only thing to do. Laura studied Hebrew. And she-- she ran to the mailman constantly for correspondence. He told her about the beauty of the land, how the main concentration is on the youth, because they are the future. It's beautiful, and you'll see. We'll have a good life. And she had tears, crying, hoping. And then, without warning, the war broke out and the Russians marched in. And Russia is isolated from the world. It's an iron curtain. She tried to smuggle out through the border, and she was caught, pushed back, sent back. Her heart broke, because she knew that she'll never see him. And after some time elapsed, correspondence stopped. If something happened to Laura, she-- she knew she's not going to see him. And she loved him very much. And my father felt very guilty. He should have-- he could have sold the house. We could have found means to send them together. But under the circumstances then, that was the rational thing to do. Had he have known what will come ahead, nothing would have stopped him to send his daughter with her husband, together. Laura's heart was broken. And now my father, when he knew that there's a chance for Christian documents-- she had a perky nose, blonde. She did not look Jewish. So-- so he knew that he must-- she must go to her husband. And he-- we have to give whatever we have. But Laura said, but you're going to be starving if you give away for our document. He said, somebody has to survive. Somebody should tell what was done to us. We thought there'll no Jews left. And your husband is waiting. I should have brought down heaven and earth and sent you together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when she saw how my father desperately wants her to live, how guilty he felt for not doing anything to help, she accepted. And then I told Mrs. [? Schubert ?] that we will-- we-- we want to obtain those documents. So what have you got to-- what have you to give?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was my mother's diamond engagement ring. She had the ring from her mother. My father had a coat with sable lined. In Europe, men used to have sable underneath, lined, which he took off the sable, rolled it up, and gave. He had an Omega watch they used to wear with a chain, a heavy, gold, 18 karat gold watch. Whatever they had. We thought that when we would be starving, that is going to be our safety valve. But my father did not think of tomorrow. The children have to survive. And because I don't look typically Jewish, I could pass as a Christian. And my younger sister, [? Rosa, ?] she's very attached to my parents. And she'll stay with us. So it was decided for-- to obtain. I went to Mrs. [? Schubert, ?] and she discussed it with them. And she told them that our things are [INAUDIBLE]]-- that Polish friend that my father-- that we gave our valuables, that he has it. And my father will send a note that they should give it to Mr. [? Jacques. ?] And I had to provide a photograph for myself and my sister. And they had to know, more or less, our age. It should correspond with the document. And it's a procedure. He's going to-- because he has to obtain a birth certificate from the christening from the church also. This is false. Everything. That I was born in another town, a different name, my mother is [INAUDIBLE] and all that, to remember. We also requested a prayer book and some information about Christianity to know. And now-- it will take about three weeks or so-- and now there are rumors that the ghetto's being erected, and we're going to go into it. Going-- they chose a Jewish-- predominantly Jewish section. And because there were so many roundups of Jews, there were empty buildings already. Children were the first ones to be caught, because they were not allowed to getting into hiding place in a cellar. If the Germans are looking, or the Ukrainians, in a roundup, the children's cry will betray the whole hiding place-- hideout place. So they were the first ones to be grabbed. So there were not too many children, too many people. And that was the section we were moving into. My family went to see how they were-- the process of-- they were erecting the ghetto. So they were drilling in posts, drilling holes, putting in posts, cementing it, and drawing with barbed wire around-- around that whole section up to-- it's a large section. And we watched. And our hearts sank when we saw. They're going to put us into a cage from which there's no return.

speaker: interviewee
text: They'll take us out, one by one, and kill us. How could people be so cruel? What right do they have to destroy our life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was young, but I was fighting to survive. I was bewildered. And I see men standing there, and they're planning. They're going to rent a little wagon to transfer their belongings, and how they were going to do. Making preparation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it incomprehensible to me. How could they calmly make plans? We're going into a cage from which they're going to take us out, one by one, and kill us. They got us in one spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I pray, please, God, give me the strength to accept the inevitable, like everybody else. I cried all the way back. And then I was thinking selfishly, those documents. In two weeks, the ghetto. We have to move into the ghetto. If the documents should come in time, before we are going in-- because I don't want to go into that cage. My two brothers worked for [INAUDIBLE] for the [INAUDIBLE]. It was set that when everybody goes into the ghetto, they are not going in. They're going to have quarters assigned for those workers, under guard. They're going to be led from and to in the city. So they're not coming in. Because they are still useful, they're going to keep them. And when the time comes, they're going to be the last one. But maybe the war's going to end. Maybe Hitler's going to be killed. There's always hope, where there's life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very nervous. Is my-- are my documents going to come? And I said to Mrs. [? Schubert. ?] And she said, I'm going to let them know they should rush up-- rush. But they couldn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: [RINGING] Oh, shit! They couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's going to show on the film? Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my documents did not come in time. We went into the ghetto thinking, is he going to find us? By the barbed wire, he's going to have to come. How is it going to be? We into the ghetto. It was several families sharing a room and a kitchen. It was a large room. When our house was destroyed, all our tenants scattered. I mean, we remained on our property, so we did not seem them. When we went into the ghetto, one of the women whose husband was taken away came to us. She wants to be with us. So we went into the ghetto, and there was about 12, 13 people in a room-- several families-- and in the center was a store, like, with a pipe leading, a wood stove, that we all have to share. We had no room, no closets. Whatever our belongings were, we slide them under the bed. There were narrow space between the beds, so we bump our heads just to take out our belongings. And we had to adjust. But that feeling, when you're fenced off, there's no escape. Whenever they're ready, they'll get us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was restless. I just cannot accept being fenced in. I want to leave. Where should we go? And if there is a roundup, can we run? There's-- there are guards.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was just one part of the ghetto [INAUDIBLE] barbed wire that it was not guarded. Besides, they were not guarded it so thoroughly, because when you have an arm band-- arm band, and when you go outside on the Christian side, Christians will point you out. Ukrainians, Christians, boys, young kids, she's Jewish! So you have no chance. They'll shoot you for being out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you take it off, where are you going? Who's going to open the door to let you in? There's no-- there's nothing to go out to. But there were some people who worked for the Germans who had a permit. At the entrance, they presented that they worked for the Germans. They could come in and out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that one part of the ghetto, as I mentioned, where the barbed wire there was spread, I could go out. But I'm afraid that some Christians will point me out. Maybe even a girl who went to school with me who will recognize me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So where am I going to go? Who's going to give me shelter? So we sit in a cage.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one man, his name was David [? Opatovsky. ?] He was a wheeler-dealer. He provided for the Germans with products, with things, whatever they wanted. He was a useful Jew. So he was on the outside. He had a permit and so. And he knew a family, [? Pavlok, ?] that-- this [? Pavlok ?] worked for some-- had a position in a hospital with some very high-ranking Germans. And his wife had a little boy, two years old. And she gave birth to a baby. She couldn't find anybody reliable that should help her-- while she came home from the hospital-- help her with the child. And there were no more Christian nannies, because every Christian, the poorest, if he gave shelter to a Jew for one week during those roundups, he was rich because the Jews had to give them money or valuable things for letting them stay. So those nannies didn't need to work anymore. Because every-- they moved into the Jewish homes. And when we were in the ghetto, all those Jewish homes, they took over.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she needed somebody for her baby. It was a little boy, few weeks old. So he came to our place, and he said-- we knew him well. He came-- he brought, like, hope, always singing. And even making jokes at our tragedy. We liked him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, there's this [? Pavlok. ?] He needs, for several weeks, a girl. She'll have good food-- not for shelter, for survival.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my Laura didn't want to go. She was-- sort of lost her will. She didn't want to go. So it was decided I go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I gathered a few of my necessary things, which I did not have much, being that our house burned down, and went to the [? Pavloks. ?] She was a good woman. I stayed there. I helped her with the little boy. I helped her with the household. And she was very unhappy that she had a boy. She wanted another girl, because she had already a little boy. But I had food. I was eating. And sometimes on Sunday, when her husband was home, she allowed me to go see my family. [PAUSES] I would-- was-- would walk through the street-- it was very dangerous-- hugging the wall where my arm band was and running. I wore a kerchief. And when I approached that side street where it was not guarded, I just spread the wires and ran in. And visited my family. It was like-- and Mrs. [? Pavlok ?] was kind. She gave me some bread, some eggs, some produce to bring to my family.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? [PAUSES] ?] Now, the-- something happened-- I'm real-- I'm going back, because it came to my memory.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, when we went into the ghetto, in the ghetto have-- an event happened. There were some young boys who didn't want to enter the ghetto. And they heard about partisans in the forests who sabotage train tracks. And they are grouping up. And those boys, two brothers, they thought perhaps if they go through the forest, they will link up with those partisans. If they have to die, at least die fighting. And they left their home, and they went to the forests, hiding out, trying to look for those partisans.

speaker: interviewee
text: But partisans are not exposed that you can see them. They are hiding from them. They don't know if they are Jews. How can they find them? They're not invisible.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at one point, they met up with another boy who told them-- who came from the ghetto, also, in the forest. And he told them that there's an epidemic-- a typhoid epidemic-- and people are dying. They are just carrying out bodies, due to lack of hygiene, lack of food. And they're keeping it secretly from the Germans, because if the Germans would know, they would put [INAUDIBLE] all around and set it on fire. It shouldn't spread.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they're laying with their-- laying in bed with their body fully-dressed. And if somebody comes, they think they're just sick. And they are moving the body at dawn. Nobody should know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there's starvation in the ghetto, and people starve. And those boys who were in the forest, who were stealing from the farmers, when they heard that, they were thinking of a way where they could bring some product to their family, risking their life going by the edge at dawn, and they're going to bring.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they hit a jackpot. They raided a barn of a farmer where they were home-baked bread and food, all the products. And they found a sack and filled it up, and they're going to bring it to their families that they shouldn't die.

speaker: interviewee
text: They walked by the edge of the forest, early in the morning. And if they would hear some vehicle, they would run in. They-- there were two Germans on motorcycles speeding. And from a distance, they noticed two bodies, two boys walking at the edge. And when the boys ran in, it was suspicious to them, because they saw them from a distance. The Germans got off their motorcycles, and they had machine guns, and went into the woods to search-- to get them. Those boys had weapons, because they hoped to join the partisan group. They had some guns. They were hiding in the woods and running. And the Germans found them. The boys were starting to shoot-- didn't aim-- and the Germans apprehended them. They left their motorcycles-- nobody's going to steal them, a German motorcycle-- and they walked, because it was near the town, walked them under-- under guard, to the Ukrainian kommandantur. The Ukrainians were helping-- they were the militia-- to bring them there, feeling they're going to take care of them. And they brought them to that Ukrainian militia, and they left. The Ukrainians were-- knew that they're Jews. They're going to send them out, and somebody who does that function will shoot them. And while the boys were leaving and crying, begging, the Ukrainians said, you have a good pair of boots-- those high boots. You don't need them. Where you're going, you don't need them. Take them off.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the boy-- the boots are very heavy, hard to remove. He was struggling, putting down. And everybody is looking at him, what he's doing. In the meantime, this German who was giving the order-- this Ukrainian, I mean, who was telling him, give me the boots, on the desk there was a gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: As the boy was taking off the boots, he noticed the gun on the edge of the desk. He grabbed the gun, shot the Ukrainian at the desk, and the other Ukrainian in the office was in such a shock that he stood. And the boys ran.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where do they run? To the ghetto where-- who's going to let them hide? Ran into the ghetto where their family was. And he stops there. Hide me. Hide me. And they told them what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a tragedy, because killing a Ukrainian is like killing a German. They are already all mobilized, even from the fire department men. They all-- they all organized and came to the ghetto, barged through the ghetto, barged into the ghetto. And people-- somebody was yelling in the distance, hide! There's going to be a pogrom! Run! Run! Because they saw them coming. They came into the ghetto, and they leaned lattice against the wall, against the windows, and climbed into people's house. Starting to kill, killing Jews. And in the ghetto, people who were walking were hacked to death by the firemen-- it was a pogrom-- killing them, looking for the boys. They should deliver the boys. Until they found the apartment where they found the boys, and they were leading them out. Knocking them all over the place. They wanted to deliver them to the authorities for killing that Ukrainian. It was a pogrom.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they left the ghetto. We-- when we heard, hide! There's a pogrom! We ran up. Where should we run? On the rooftop.

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, we were afraid to look down. They might see us. And so we were on the roof lying flat, waiting for them to come and get us. At some point, we-- somebody looked up, like lifted his head to look. And they saw Ukrainians and a German leading out two boys, were leaving. Then we knew. We had a feeling that that's over. They left the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came down, and people were gathering. What happened? They told us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we saw people killed, bodies, blood spattered. And from the Judenrat, from the committee, they came and told us about the boys who were partisans, that all they wanted was to survive. But they brought food to their parents, and they brought disaster on us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Instead of people praising those boys, who risked their life to bring food to their parents, we cursed them because they brought a pogrom on us. We cursed them. Why did they run to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we could not afford heroes at that time. [PAUSES] After that pogrom, we were wiser people. Seeing all that, we knew that there's no hope. Now, I'm going back to the time where I was out of the ghetto to go to [? Pavlok, ?] to be at [? Pavlok's ?] house. And I would come in and bring some foods. And there was an epidemic-- typhoid epidemic. And my younger sister, [? Rosa, ?] was sick. And my mother did not even have any milk, any juice, anything to give her. Before the war, my mother used to pay us for drinking milk. And now she saw her-- when the crisis passed and the fever was down and she was pale, all we had was water for her. So it was necessary to bring some food, that I risked my life, going, hugging the wall, bringing some food for my parents, sneaking in. And every time I left, my father-- my parents cry, because we didn't know we're going to see each other. But I knew that a few weeks pass, I'm going to come in. And so-- but we're-- the realization after that pogrom was such that there was no more hope. I remember when I was in the ghetto, I used to go by the fence and look outside. Look-- from a distance, we could see the Christian section. It's like being in a cage. And we see there was a girl, mentally retarded, [INAUDIBLE]. She was harmless, dancing in the streets and singing. Somebody said she had a disappointed love, and she lost her reason. We saw from a distance-- from the marketplace there, from our ghetto-- Germans throwing coins at her while she was dancing. I envied her, because they like her. They give her-- they are not going to kill her. She could go into the field and lie down and have-- feel the warmth of the sun without fear. I just ran away from there. I did not like to go to the edge of the ghetto and see that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes people said that planes should come down and kill us all, just like Samson and the temple where he died with the Philistines. If the bombs should destroy the ghetto and all of us-- with the Germans together. I used to walk away from the ghetto, from the fence, crying. And I said, why am I fighting it so? Why can't I accept the inevitable, like everybody else?
